CalifOrnia
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornmission
June 20, 1990
D. willian Heine
schwartz, Steinsapir, Dohrmann & Sommers
3580 wil-shire Boulevard, suite 1820
Los Angeles, CA 90010-2594
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. A-90-37o
Dear Mr. Heine:
This is in response to your reguest for advice on behalf of
the Democratic Agenda For the 90's (the rrAgendar') with respect to
the contribution limitations of the Po1itical Reform Act (the
ra"grt 1 . 1
This letter represents conservative advice which wifl be
subrnitted to the cornmission for revj.ew at a future meeting.
OUESTION
can the Agenda 1awfu1ly transfer money which has been brought
into compliance with the contributj.on linits of the Act fron its
non-candj-date account to its candidate account, and then use that
noney to make contributions to candidates?
CONCLUS ION
Funds received in excess of the contribution l-inits and
placed in an account to be used for purposes other than making
contributions to candidates cannot be divided at some l-ater time
into contributions to candidate and non-candidate accounts.
FACTS
The Agenda, a recipient committee, has an account which has
been used to collect and disburse lnoney for use in voter registra-
tion and get-out-the-vote drives, but not to rnake contributions to
candidates (the rrnon-candidate accountrr) ' 
The Agenda is in the
,l' 
covernment code Sections 81000-91015. A1l- statutory references
are to the covernment Code unLess otherwi.se indicated. 
cornrnission
reguJ.ations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations section
18OOO, et seq. Al1 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the Californj.a code of Regul-ations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 . Sacramento CA 958O4.O8O7 . (916)322.5660


Fi■e NO. A-90-370
Page 2
process of establishi.ng another accoult _which will be used to make
Lontributions to candidates (the rrcandidate accountrr).
You wish to know if funds in the non-candidate account rnay be
transferred to the candidate account, provided the transferred
money j.s brought into conformity with the fiscal year contribution
limils of the Act. 
You proPose the followingl procedure to ac-
conpl-ish this:
1. The Agenda will establish a separate account for transfer
of contrj.butions deened in coropliance with the contribution limits
of the Act.
2. The Agenda will presune that the total amount of cash and
cash equivalents on hand in its non-candidate account when the
""o".a{e bank account is established consists of contributi'ons
nolt recently received. Loans receivable shalL be included in the
total of cash and cash equivalents.
3. The Agenda wil-l revj.ew contributions received by the non-
candj.date account beginning r^rith the fast contribution received
and working back in tine until the total amount of cash and cash
equivalentl on hand is reached. Shoutd the review indicate that
ariy contributions to the non-candidate account exceed the ap-
olicable lirnitations set forth in the Act, the total amount of
Lash and cash equivafents that wil-I be deposited in or all-ocated
to the candidate account witl be reduced by the amount in excess
of the applicable contribution 1j-nits.
4- In determining whether a contribution is in compliance
with the limits, the Agenda shal1 aggregate the total amount of
contributions received in the fiscal year from each person,
political 
conmittee, broad-based political 
committee, and
political 
PartY.
5. The contributions lthich are brought into compliance with
the linits 
r,ri11 be deposited in the account for use in naking
contributions to candidates. A11 Agenda contributions to
candidates will comply with Sections 85303 and 85305 of the Act
and the regulations Pronulgated thereunder.
6. The Agenda $riIf report on its carnpaign statements all
contributions deposited in and expenditures made from the
candidate account.
ANALYSIS
The Act linits the amount that any person may contribute to
committee for the Purpose of roaking contributions to candidates,
as follosrs:
No person sha1l make and no political 
committee,
broad based political 
cornmittee, or political party
shal1 solicit or accept, any contribution or Ioan from a


Fi■e No. A-90-370
Page 3
person whi-ch rrould cause the total amount contributed or
loaned by that person to the same political 
cornmittee,
broad based political 
committee, or politica] party to
exceed two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) in any
fiscal year to make contributions to candidates for
elective office.
Section 85302, emphasis added.
The Act further provides that the linitation 
on contributions
to political 
comnittees does not apply to |tf inancial or other sup-
port to one or more polj.tical committees or broad based political
Lomrnittees provided the supnort is used for purposes other than
(SeCtion 85303(c), emphasis added.)
Based upon the foregoingt, we have advised that contributions
to poLitical committees are not subject to the contributj-on
:-irnits, provided the contributions are not for the purpose of mak-
ing contiibutions to candidates. (calvert Advice Letter, No.
A-089-403, copy enclosed.) Horever, we have previously advised
that this applies only when the contributions received by the
committee aie not commingled with funds used to make contributions
to candidates. (Dowe11 Advice Letter, No. A-89-566, copy
encl,osed. )
In December of 1989, the Commission adopted Regulation 185352
(copy enclosed) , which interpreted Section 85303 (c) and outlined
the- Lircurnstances perrnitting contributions in excess of the
linits. 
That regulation provides that contributions received by a
political 
committee in excess of the contribution limits must be
deposited in a separate account which does not include funds used
to nake contributions to candidates. (Regulation 18535(a) (2) -)
The funds in that account may be expended onfv for purposes other
than to rnake contributions to candidates for elective office.
(Regulation 18535(a) (3).) 
A contribution received in excess of
the li:nits nust be returned unless the portion j.n excess of the
contribution linits 
has been earnarked for use other than naking
contributions to candidates either in a written solicitation 
for
contributions by the political 
connittee or in a written document
received from the contributor at or near the time of the contribu-
tion. 
(Regulation 18535 (c) . )
According to the facts stated in your letter, the funds which
you propose to segregate into a candidate account are presently
t'comrningled" lrith funds recei.ved in excess of the linits. 
You do
not indicate that there is any written record establishing the
contributor's intent that the funds be allocated between a
candidate and non-candidate account. Moreover, since the making
2 Regulation L8535
currently effective, is in the rulemaking process.
the regulation expresses the
Although not
policy of the


Fi■e No. A-90-370
Page 4
or soLici.tation of a contribution in excess of the l-irnits for
purposes of naking contributions to candidates would violate the
irct, it is more 1ife:.y than not that there was either an express
or inplied understanding, at the time a contribution in excess of
the l-irnits was solicited and made, that it eroul'd not be used to
rnake contributions to candidates.
Section 85303 (c) represents an exception to the general
thrust of Proposition 73 to lini't carnpaign contributions and
should be nariowlv construed. It sinply prohibits using the
contribution linits 
imposed by Proposition z3 to linit 
a person/s
ability to provide financial support to a political 
cornmittee for
p"ip"=i,= otirer than to make contributions to candidates. Nothing
in section provides for use of sone portion of such financial sup-
port to make contributions to candidates.
The present po).icy adopted by the Cornmission requiring writ-
ten identification 
and segregation of funds between candidate and
non-candidate accounts at or near the time of receipt represents a
narrow construction of section 85303 (c) . It permits a Person to
*ii" u contribution within the linits to a politj.cal cornmittee for
use in making contributions to candidates and to contribute excess
funds for purposes other than rnaking contributions to candidates.
i"r"""., 
firnd-s received in excess of the lirnits and commingled in
an account which is used for purposes other than making contribu-
iions to candidates may not be used to make contributions to
candidates. Therefore, we would conservativeJ.y advj.se that funds
received in excess of the contribution linits 
and placed in.an
iccount to be used for purposes other than naking contributions to
candidates cannot be divided at sone Later time into contributions
to candidate and non-candidate accounts.
As indicated above, this letter represents conservative
advice which r,ril1 be subnitted to the Comrnission for review'
ifr""fa the Commission adopt a broader interpretation of Sect1on
eigo3 t"l which would permit the segregation of funds, we believe
that the nethod proposed by you for accomplishing the allocation
to the candidate actount is a reasonable one. Ho!'ever, one ad-
Jiti"""f 
step would be required in order to assure compliance with
the Act. Settion 85304 prohibits transfers of funds between
candidates or their controlled conmittees. This has been
interpreted as prohibiting candidates from indirectly transferring
contributions to other candidates by making contributions to
political 
conmj.ttees for the purPose of making contributions to
^candidates. (calvert Advice Letter, suDra. ) Therefore, any funds
included in the funds subject to transfer to the candidate account
must be reduced by any amount which represents a contributj'on to
the cornmittee from a candidate.


Fi■e No. A-90-370
Page 5
SH:MWE:gb
Enc■oSure
If you have any questions regardi-ng the above, you may
contact ne at (915) 322-5901-
sincerely,
scott HaLfabrin
Acting ceneraL counsel
4/t%
By: Margaret w. Ellison
Counsel, LegaL Division
